Document [26] (Title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Cellular) The most recently elucidated epigenetic mechanism is non-coding RNA-associated gene silencing. A non-coding RNA (ncRNA) is a functional RNA molecule that is transcribed but not translated into proteins. Once regarded as waste of the genome, recent insight suggests the ncRNA molecules harbor a crucial role in epigenetic gene expression and likely account for the great difference in phenotype between species and within human populations despite such similarity in encoded proteins. [6] [18] Notable ncRNA molecules include microRNAs (miRNA) and short interfering RNAs (siRNA), which include less than 30 nucleotides, and long non-coding RNAs (lncRNA), which are 200 nucleotides or longer. Though the full extent of their role in epigenetics is still being determined, there is evidence suggesting that ncRNAs participate in DNA methylation and histone modifications in addition to gene silencing. [20] siRNAs and lncRNAs both have been shown to regulate gene expression by the formation of heterochromatin. [6] [21]

Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 314) Amino acids and derivatives comprise several biologically important compounds, including L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, and glutamine, each of which is classified as a member of this chemical group. These molecules play key roles in cellular metabolism, energy production, methyl‑group transfer, and nitrogen balance, making them essential for normal physiological function and for the management of various metabolic disorders. In contrast, metreleptin, a recombinant leptin analog used to treat leptin deficiency and related metabolic conditions, does not belong to the amino acids and derivatives category; it is a protein‑based therapeutic rather than an amino‑acid‑derived compound. Recognizing the correct classification of these entities supports accurate understanding of their mechanisms of action and appropriate clinical application.
Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1557) S‑adenosylmethionin (S‑adenosylmethionine) and betain are both classified as members of the broader biochemical group “Amino acids and derivatives.” In addition, the therapeutic protein metreleptin is also considered a member of this “Amino acids and derivatives” category, reflecting its composition of amino‑acid residues. Recognizing that S‑adenosylmethionin, betain, and metreleptin share this common classification highlights their related metabolic origins and can inform their roles in physiological processes and potential therapeutic use.

Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895) S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) and glutamine are both classified as members of the broader chemical group “amino acids and derivatives,” indicating that they share the fundamental structural features of amino acids and related compounds. Recognizing S‑adenosylmethionine and glutamine as amino‑acid derivatives helps clinicians and researchers understand their functions, interactions, and potential therapeutic relevance within the context of amino‑acid metabolism.

Document [15] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1420) Amino acids and derivatives constitute a broad chemical class that includes biologically important molecules such as S‑adenosylmethionin, a methyl‑group donor derived from the amino acid methionine. The therapeutic protein metreleptin, a recombinant leptin analog used to treat leptin deficiency, is also built from amino‑acid building blocks, linking it to the same class of amino‑acid‑derived compounds. Together, these relationships illustrate how both small‑molecule metabolites like S‑adenosylmethionin and larger biologic agents such as metreleptin are rooted in the fundamental chemistry of amino acids and their derivatives, underscoring the central role of this class in metabolism and drug development.
Document [16] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1902) L‑karnitin, S‑adenosylmethionin, and glutamin are all classified as members of the broader category “Amino acids and derivatives.” This grouping includes both standard amino acids and related compounds that serve essential roles in metabolism, such as energy transport (L‑karnitin), methyl group donation (S‑adenosylmethionin), and nitrogen balance and protein synthesis (glutamin). Recognizing that these three substances belong to the Amino acids and derivatives class helps clinicians and researchers appreciate their shared biochemical context and informs their use in therapeutic and nutritional strategies.
